title: Unaccompanied Children Program Foundational Rule; Sponsor Assessment Update To Include Proof of Identity, Background Check, Placement, and Income Verification Standards
latest_action: Proposed Rule published in Federal Register
sponsor: Health and Human Services Department, Children and Families Administration
summary: This notice of proposed rulemaking (NPRM, or proposed rule) would establish certain additional requirements for sponsor suitability assessments related to proof of identity, proof of income, and other information required for background checks to promote the safe placement of unaccompanied alien children (UAC). This NPRM proposes acceptable documentation for proof of identity and would require pro
description: This notice of proposed rulemaking (NPRM, or proposed rule) would establish certain additional requirements for sponsor suitability assessments related to proof of identity, proof of income, and other information required for background checks to promote the safe placement of unaccompanied alien children (UAC). This NPRM proposes acceptable documentation for proof of identity and would require proof of income from potential sponsors of UAC in ORR custody by reason of their immigration status, as described in the Homeland Security Act of 2002 (HSA) and the William Wilberforce Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act of 2008 (TVPRA). This NPRM also proposes amendments to background check requirements for sponsor suitability assessments and the conduct of examinations of UAC related to considerations of UAC dangerousness to self or others that align with the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Finally, this NPRM proposes certain administrative updates to align numbering and terminology between proposals and existing regulations. The docket on https://www.regulations.gov will include a plain language summary of the NPRM.

updates.event: Proposed Rule published: Unaccompanied Children Program Foundational Rule; Sponsor Assessment Update To I
updates.actor: Health and Human Services Department, Children and Families Administration

title: Notice of Determinations and Request for Comments Concerning Actions in Section 301 Investigations of Acts, Policies, and Practices of Various Economies Related to the Failure To Impose and Effectively Enforce a Prohibition on the Importation of Goods Produced With Forced Labor
sponsor: Trade Representative, Office of United States
summary: On March 12, 2026, the United States Trade Representative (Trade Representative) initiated 60 investigations related to the failure of various economies to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor. The Trade Representative has determined that 54 of the investigated economies have failed to impose and effectively enforce a forced labor impo
description: On March 12, 2026, the United States Trade Representative (Trade Representative) initiated 60 investigations related to the failure of various economies to impose and effectively enforce a prohibition on the importation of goods produced with forced labor. The Trade Representative has determined that 54 of the investigated economies have failed to impose and effectively enforce a forced labor import prohibition. The Trade Representative has determined that six of the investigated economies have failed to effectively enforce a forced labor import prohibition. The Trade Representative has determined that the failure of each of the investigated economies to impose and effectively enforce a force labor import prohibition is unreasonable and burdens or restricts U.S. commerce. As a result of the findings in each investigation, the Trade Representative proposes that appropriate action includes additional duties on all products of the investigated economies, except as provided in Annex A to this Notice. For economies that impose a forced labor import prohibition; have taken on commitments related to forced labor import prohibitions through an Agreement on Reciprocal Trade; or have imposed a partial regime with the effect of preventing the importation of certain forced labor goods, the Trade Representative proposes 10% as the rate of additional duties. For all other economies, the Trade Representative proposes 12.5% as the rate of additional duties. The Trade Representative also proposes a textile mechanism that would allow for a certain volume of apparel and textile imports from certain economies to enter the United States at a reduced Section 301 tariff rate. USTR seeks public comments on the proposed actions in the investigations and will hold public hearings in connection with the proposals.

updates.event: Notice published: Notice of Determinations and Request for Comments Concerning Actions in Section
updates.actor: Trade Representative, Office of United States

title: Solicitation of Proposals for Annual Combating Human Trafficking in Transportation Impact Awards
summary: The annual Combating Human Trafficking in Transportation Impact Awards (the awards) seek to raise awareness among transportation stakeholders about human trafficking and increase training and prevention to combat the crime. The awards are a component of the Department of Transportation (DOT) Transportation Leaders Against Human Trafficking initiative. Additional information regarding the Departmen
description: The annual Combating Human Trafficking in Transportation Impact Awards (the awards) seek to raise awareness among transportation stakeholders about human trafficking and increase training and prevention to combat the crime. The awards are a component of the Department of Transportation (DOT) Transportation Leaders Against Human Trafficking initiative. Additional information regarding the Department's counter-trafficking activities can be found at www.transportation.gov/stophumantrafficking. The awards serve as an incentive for transportation stakeholders to creatively develop impactful and innovative counter-trafficking tools, initiatives, campaigns, and technologies that can help stop these heinous crimes, and as a platform to publicize and share the success of those efforts. The awards are open to individuals and entities, including non- governmental organizations, transportation industry associations, research institutions, and state and local government entities and organizations. Entrants compete for three cash awards: up to $100,000 for first place, up to $50,000 for second place, and up to $25,000 for third place. These prizes are to be awarded to the individual(s) or entities selected for creating the most impactful and innovative counter-trafficking initiative or technology. DOT seeks to incentivize individuals and entities to think creatively in developing innovative solutions to combat human trafficking in the transportation industry, and to share those innovations with the broader community.

updates.event: Notice published: Solicitation of Proposals for Annual Combating Human Trafficking in Transportati

title: Standards of Conduct; Revocation of Superseded Regulations; Revision of Residual Provisions
sponsor: Health and Human Services Department, Office of the Secretary
summary: The Department of Health and Human Services (Department or HHS) is revising and reissuing the Standards of Conduct, a set of substantive and procedural rules relating to conduct and employee responsibilities that augment the Standards of Ethical Conduct for Employees of the Executive Branch, the Supplemental Standards of Ethical Conduct for Employees of the Department of Health and Human Services,
description: The Department of Health and Human Services (Department or HHS) is revising and reissuing the Standards of Conduct, a set of substantive and procedural rules relating to conduct and employee responsibilities that augment the Standards of Ethical Conduct for Employees of the Executive Branch, the Supplemental Standards of Ethical Conduct for Employees of the Department of Health and Human Services, the Supplemental Financial Disclosure Requirements for Employees of the Department of Health and Human Services, the Employee Responsibilities and Conduct Regulation, and the Executive Branch Financial Disclosure regulations. The Department is removing provisions that have been superseded by these regulations or are otherwise obsolete or unnecessary to efficient administration. This final rule addresses conduct on Federal Government (Government) property and the use of Government funds or official information; restates existing standards for workplace courtesy; specifies rules for acceptance of gifts, travel, and employment from foreign governments and other non- Federal entities; provides notice of disciplinary actions available to address violations and prescribes the continuing employee obligation to report violations of rules or law to appropriate authorities. This revision adds a new section addressing Counter-Trafficking in Persons requirements in response to the Trafficking Victims Prevention and Protection Reauthorization Act of 2022 (Pub. L. 117-348). The rule also continues and delineates restrictions on the political activity of commissioned officers of the United States Public Health Service, a category of employees not covered by the Hatch Act Reform Amendments of 1993, as amended.

updates.event: Rule published: Standards of Conduct; Revocation of Superseded Regulations; Revision of Residual
updates.actor: Health and Human Services Department, Office of the Secretary

title: Trafficking Survivors Economic Empowerment Act (TSEEA) — Proposed
latest_action: Advocacy coalition circulating draft text; sponsor recruitment ongoing.
sponsor: TBD — Advocacy coalition seeking lead sponsor
summary: Proposed legislation that would mandate economic empowerment outcomes measurement in federally-funded trafficking programs. Requires tracking of employment placements, income levels, credit access, and housing stability — creating the first federal requirement for long-term survivor economic outcome data.
description: Draft provisions include: (1) Mandate employment placement, income stability, and credit access metrics for all federally-funded trafficking programs; (2) Create new grant category for survivor-led economic empowerment programs; (3) Require 12-month follow-up tracking for program participants; (4) Establish outcome benchmarks and reporting requirements; (5) Create survivor economic empowerment data dashboard at HHS. Being developed by coalition including Polaris Project, Freedom Network USA, and survivor advocacy organizations.

updates.event: Initial concept paper circulated by advocacy coalition

updates.notes: Circulated to Congressional staff for feedback

updates.event: Sponsor recruitment ongoing; 3 House members considering introducing

latest_action: Introduced in House. Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce.
summary: Extends federal funding for the National Human Trafficking Hotline through FY2028 and requires annual reporting on call volume by state, trafficking type, referral outcomes, and demographic data, published in machine-readable format.
description: Authorizes $10M annually through FY2028 for the National Human Trafficking Hotline (operated by Polaris Project under HHS contract). New requirements: call volume by state, trafficking type breakdown, referral outcome tracking, demographic data on callers. Mandates HHS to publish data in machine-readable format. Requires biennial program evaluation.

title: FinCEN Rule Clarification: Beneficial Ownership & Shell Company Nexus to Human Trafficking
latest_action: Final rule clarification published. Compliance date: 90 days from publication (May 29, 2025).
summary: Clarifies how the Corporate Transparency Act's beneficial ownership database can be used to investigate shell companies linked to trafficking networks. Sets procedures for law enforcement access to BOI data in trafficking investigations.
description: Supplements CTA implementing regulations to clarify law enforcement access for investigating shell companies linked to trafficking. Includes: (1) Procedures for submitting beneficial ownership data requests in trafficking investigations; (2) Data retention requirements for trafficking-related BOI queries; (3) Coordination procedures with DOJ and DHS for cross-border networks; (4) Guidance on identifying shell company indicators within trafficking financial flows.

updates.event: Final rule clarification published in Federal Register
updates.notes: 90-day compliance clock begins

upcoming.event: Compliance deadline — financial institutions must update BOI query procedures

description: Key provisions: (1) Increases federal mandatory minimums for trafficking offenses; (2) Expands HHS grant programs for transitional housing; (3) Requires DOJ to collect and publish annual prosecution data disaggregated by race, ethnicity, gender, and trafficking type; (4) Creates new survivor advisory councils within federal agencies; (5) Funds survivor-led research on long-term outcomes; (6) Adds new requirements for federally-funded shelter providers.

title: Trafficking Survivors Relief Act (TSRA) — Record Vacatur/Expungement
status: Referred to Judiciary; House Judiciary Committee hearing Sep 2025
latest_action: Referred to Judiciary; House Judiciary Committee hearing Sep 2025
cosponsor_list: Hank Johnson [D-GA] + 8 others (bipartisan, 7R 4D) — SIGNED INTO LAW P.L. 119-73
summary: Federal expungement and vacatur of convictions for crimes committed due to trafficking; affirmative defense; DOJ grants for legal representation
description: HIGH PRIORITY SURVIVOR BILL: Would remove criminal record barrier to housing, employment, education. GAO study would produce first federal evaluation of record-relief program impact. Passed House Judiciary in 118th but not enacted.
evaluation_required: Yes — GAO study 3 years; AG training report 1 year

specific_metrics: Prosecutions; T-visas; services funded; policy reforms enacted

summary: HSTFs in every state; coordinate HT, smuggling, and immigration enforcement; Operation Justice for All (Aug 2025)

summary: Annual assessment of U.S. anti-trafficking efforts; documents 2024-2025 policy changes
description: Documents Jan 2025 disruptions (stop work orders; deferred action; fear of deportation). Notes LGBTQIA removal from assessment. Missing: 2025 report not released by Jun 30 deadline — major gap.

title: FinCEN Advisory: Identifying and Reporting Human Trafficking (FIN-2024-A008)
latest_action: Advisory issued. Financial institutions advised to update SAR filing procedures to include new labor trafficking typologies.
summary: Updated SAR filing guidance with new typologies for labor trafficking in agriculture, domestic work, hospitality, and construction. Includes updated red-flag indicators for prepaid cards, wire transfers, and cryptocurrency associated with trafficking proceeds.
description: This advisory updates FinCEN's 2014 guidance on human trafficking. Key additions: (1) New labor trafficking typologies for hospitality, agriculture, domestic work, and construction; (2) Specific red-flag indicators for prepaid cards, wire transfers, and cryptocurrency; (3) Updated guidance on geographic clustering indicators; (4) New section on online recruitment platforms and advertising; (5) Enhanced guidance for commercial sex-related SARs. All SAR narratives should reference FIN-2024-A008 when related activity is detected.

updates.event: Original FinCEN advisory on human trafficking issued (FIN-2014-A008)
updates.notes: Original guidance that this advisory replaces

updates.event: Updated advisory FIN-2024-A008 issued — replaces 2014 guidance
updates.notes: 10-year update reflects evolved trafficking methods and new financial technologies

title: CFPB Rule: Adverse Credit Reporting Prohibition for Trafficking Survivors
latest_action: Rule effective. Trafficking survivors may request removal of adverse credit items resulting from trafficking.
summary: Amends FCRA to implement JVTA requirement that trafficking survivors can block adverse credit reporting items resulting from their trafficking. Credit bureaus must establish procedures for survivor requests. Enforcement status uncertain post-2025 under CFPB leadership changes.
description: Implements § 155 of the Justice for Victims of Trafficking Act, requiring credit reporting agencies to block adverse items resulting from trafficking. Key provisions: (1) Survivors may submit trafficking documentation to credit bureaus to block adverse items; (2) Credit bureaus must respond within 4 business days; (3) Establishes documentation requirements (court records, law enforcement certification, HHS letter); (4) Prevents re-reporting of blocked items. Note: CFPB enforcement capacity and budget have been significantly reduced under 2025 administration changes.

updates.notes: 60-day public comment period opened

updates.event: Public comment period closes — 2,400+ comments received
CFPB enforcement activities paused under new leadership
updates.notes: Enforcement uncertainty following DOGE-related CFPB restructuring; rule remains on books but active enforcement unclear

upcoming.event: Congressional oversight hearing on CFPB rule enforcement expected
upcoming.notes: Pending advocacy pressure from survivor organizations

title: T Nonimmigrant Status Final Rule (DHS)
summary: Modifies T-visa program; clarifies reporting requirements; updates bona fide determination; extended deferred action 2→4 years (labor violation victims)

title: Pennsylvania SB 44 — Minor Sex Trafficking Definition Expansion
summary: Eliminates third-party control requirement for minor CSEC definition; all minors in commercial sex recognized as trafficking victims

specific_metrics: Children identified as victims (not offenders); services accessed

summary: Requires establishment of multidisciplinary HT response teams (MDTs) for collaborative survivor-centered response
description: STATE MODEL: MDTs = DOJ best practice. Collaborative model between LE, child welfare, service providers. Framework for survivor-centered response.
implementing_agency: VA child welfare; law enforcement; victim services
specific_metrics: MDTs established; collaborations formed

summary: Clarifies reporting requirements; bona fide determination; extends deferred action 2→4 years for labor violation victims
description: 2024 rule improved process. Jan 2025: categorical deferred action terminated. Anti-trafficking experts report victims remaining in trafficking situations due to deportation fears.

title: Polaris Resilience Fund — Direct Cash Assistance Pilot
status: Active — 18-month pilot; Cohort 1 at month 9 (2024 mid-eval); Cohort 2 in design
latest_action: Active — 18-month pilot; Cohort 1 at month 9 (2024 mid-eval); Cohort 2 in design
summary: Direct unrestricted cash assistance ($500/month for 18 months) to trafficking survivors; trust-based model; no conditions on how funds are used
description: Cohort 1: N=24. At enrollment: 43% earning ≤$25K/year (vs 26% U.S. average). 9-month mid-eval shows improving financial stability, credit scores, housing. Full 18-month final evaluation expected 2025. Most rigorous survivor economic outcome pilot in field. Survivors involved in program design via Advisory Council.
evaluation_required: Yes — mid-program evaluation published (9-month); final 18-month evaluation planned
specific_metrics: Monthly financial stability diary; credit score (pre/post); housing stability measure (self-report); mental health service access; income level; bill-payment ability; food security; trust in program
survivor_outcomes_possible: Yes — designed for LT economic + wellbeing outcomes

title: Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act of 2022
latest_action: Signed into law by President Biden (P.L. 117-347)
summary: 5-year TVPA reauthorization extending funding through FY2027. Added new provisions for trafficking data collection, enhanced survivor services, expanded safe harbor for minors, strengthened international supply chain accountability, and created new grants for survivor-led research.
description: Key additions in the 2022 reauthorization: (1) Extended authorizations through FY2027; (2) Required HHS to issue guidance on online recruitment and identification; (3) Strengthened requirements for disaggregated data collection by DOJ; (4) Created new grant programs for survivor-led research partnerships; (5) Added provisions requiring federal contractors to certify supply chain due diligence; (6) Expanded role of survivor advisory boards in federal agency decision-making; (7) Enhanced training requirements for frontline workers (health care, transportation, hospitality).

upcoming.notes: Congress will need to pass TVPRA 2027 or new authorizations lapse

title: Uyghur Forced Labor Prevention Act
latest_action: Signed into law by President Biden (P.L. 117-78)
summary: Creates a rebuttable presumption that goods made wholly or in part in Xinjiang, China, or by entities on the UFLPA Entity List, are produced with forced labor and are prohibited from US import. Administered by CBP.
description: UFLPA establishes a rebuttable presumption that goods from Xinjiang are produced with forced labor and are therefore prohibited from US import under 19 U.S.C. § 1307. Key provisions: (1) CBP must enforce the presumption; (2) Importers can rebut the presumption only with 'clear and convincing evidence'; (3) Created UFLPA Entity List of companies complicit in forced labor; (4) Required interagency strategy including DHS, USTR, Commerce, State, Labor; (5) Mandated annual reporting to Congress; (6) Impacts particularly cotton, polysilicon, tomatoes, and aluminum from Xinjiang. Major supply chain compliance implications for US importers.

updates.notes: Overwhelmingly bipartisan

updates.event: UFLPA enforcement strategy released; CBP begins enforcement
updates.notes: 180-day implementation period elapsed; enforcement began with interagency coordination

updates.event: First annual report to Congress submitted
updates.notes: DHS reported over 4,000 enforcement actions in first year; 16 entities on Entity List

updates.event: UFLPA Entity List expanded to 68 entities
updates.notes: Additions include semiconductor, battery, and automotive supply chain companies

status: Enacted; CFPB rule 2022; advisory opinion Jan 2024

title: FOSTA-SESTA: Allow States and Victims to Fight Online Sex Trafficking Act / Stop Enabling Sex Traffickers Act
latest_action: Signed into law by President Trump (P.L. 115-164)
committee: Senate Commerce, Science, and Transportation Committee
summary: Amended Section 230 of the CDA to allow sex trafficking survivors to sue online platforms that knowingly facilitated their trafficking. Led to immediate shutdown of Backpage.com. Highly controversial — research shows mixed impact on safety of sex workers.
description: FOSTA-SESTA carved out a trafficking exception to Section 230 immunity for online platforms. Key effects: (1) Created 18 U.S.C. § 2421A, criminalizing platforms that 'promote or facilitate prostitution' or 'recklessly disregard' sex trafficking; (2) Allowed state AGs and survivors to bring civil claims against platforms; (3) Led to immediate shutdown of Backpage.com, Craigslist Personals, and dozens of platforms; (4) Documented adverse effects on sex worker safety by pushing activity offline and underground; (5) Subject of ongoing legal challenges and proposed amendment legislation (SAFE Sex Workers Study Act). Frequently cited as example of trafficking law with unintended consequences.

updates.notes: Senators Paul and Wyden cast the two 'no' votes

updates.event: DOJ seizes Backpage.com and arrests founders — one day before presidential signing
updates.notes: Backpage had been the largest online platform for sex trafficking and adult advertising in the US

title: Allow States to Fight Online Sex Trafficking Act (FOSTA-SESTA)
summary: Amends Section 230 CDA; civil and state liability for platforms facilitating sex trafficking
description: CRITICAL EXAMPLE: GAO found minimal prosecutions. Research documents harm to sex workers and no evidence of reduced trafficking. Best 'unintended consequences' case study.
specific_metrics: Prosecutions; platform takedowns; CSAM reports; civil damages
adaptation_risk: YES — documented platform displacement; underground migration; increased sex worker violence

title: California Safe Harbor Act (SB 1322)
latest_action: Signed into law by Governor Brown
summary: Landmark state legislation prohibiting the arrest and prosecution of minors for commercial sex acts. Established that minors engaged in sex work are victims requiring services, not criminals. Cited as model for other state safe harbor bills.
description: SB 1322 amended California Penal Code to prohibit arrest and prosecution of persons under 18 for engaging in commercial sex acts. Key provisions: (1) Decriminalized commercial sex acts for minors — law enforcement required to treat minors as victims; (2) Required law enforcement to provide access to survivor services; (3) Created diversion pathways rather than detention for minors; (4) Established interagency task force coordination requirements; (5) Cited in 40+ states as model legislation for safe harbor approaches. California became the first large state to fully decriminalize commercial sex acts for minors.

updates.event: Signed into law by Governor Jerry Brown

updates.notes: California becomes first large state to fully decriminalize commercial sex for minors

title: SOAR Health and Wellness Training Program
summary: Train healthcare and social service providers to identify and respond to trafficking; nationally accredited
description: Training/output model. No evidence that SOAR training translates to improved survivor outcomes. Important to flag 'training ≠ impact' gap in literature review.
evaluation_required: Yes — training delivery metrics; certification data
specific_metrics: Providers trained; certifications issued; training completions

title: SOAR Health & Wellness Training Program (HHS/OTIP)
summary: Online training for healthcare providers, social workers, law enforcement on identifying and responding to trafficking; continuing education credits
description: Knowledge metrics: pre/post tests administered. No tracking of whether SOAR-trained providers actually identify more victims or connect them to better services. Classic training output measurement problem.
implementing_agency: HHS/OTIP; healthcare systems; schools; law enforcement agencies
specific_metrics: Providers/trainees completing SOAR (count); certification rates; training module completions; pre/post knowledge test scores
survivor_outcomes_possible: No — training output only; no tracking of whether trained providers identify more victims
focus_question: training output metrics only; good example of provider-level vs. survivor-level measurement gap

title: Justice for Victims of Trafficking Act of 2015
latest_action: Signed into law by President Obama (P.L. 114-22)
summary: Created the Domestic Trafficking Victims' Fund, established new tools to prosecute traffickers and buyers, strengthened demand-side enforcement, required sex offender registration for trafficking convictions, and expanded restitution for survivors.
description: Key provisions: (1) Created Domestic Trafficking Victims' Fund, funded by fines on trafficking-related convictions; (2) Required convicted traffickers to pay into survivor restitution funds; (3) Added 'buyers' of sex from trafficking victims to federal prosecution targets; (4) Required sex offender registration for trafficking convictions; (5) Directed HHS to establish a grant program for survivors; (6) Required federal law enforcement to report trafficking data annually; (7) Improved coordination between child welfare and law enforcement systems.

updates.notes: Strong bipartisan vote

updates.event: Passed House 420–4 after months of hold due to abortion language dispute
updates.notes: Hold by Democrats was resolved via compromise on victim fund restrictions

title: Justice for Victims of Trafficking Act (JVTA)
summary: CSEC redefined as child abuse; Domestic Trafficking Victims Fund; survivor Advisory Council; buyer liability
description: Established U.S. Advisory Council on Human Trafficking (survivor-led). Required states to classify CSEC as child abuse. Created fund from buyer fines.
evaluation_required: Yes — Domestic Trafficking Victims Fund reporting
specific_metrics: Cases prosecuted; services funded; fund collections

title: Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Updates — Anti-Trafficking in Contractor Supply Chains
summary: Zero-tolerance for trafficking in federal contractor supply chains; compliance plans; anti-trafficking training; air carrier training provisions
description: Airline training provisions particularly relevant for transportation-sector trafficking identification. Compliance/output model throughout.
specific_metrics: Contractor compliance plans; training delivered; violations reported

title: Domestic Victims of Human Trafficking (DVHT) Demonstration Program
summary: Comprehensive services for domestic trafficking survivors: housing, employment, behavioral health
description: Federal evaluation documents service delivery challenges. Long-term survivor outcomes NOT consistently captured. Gap between program theory of change and measurement.
specific_metrics: Service delivery documentation; barriers documented; grantee practices

title: Combat Human Trafficking Act of 2015 — BJS Reporting Requirement
summary: Requires BJS to collect and report data on human trafficking annually
description: Foundation for BJS annual data reports. 2024 report: 1,939 in state prison for HT; 1,070 defendants charged in federal court FY2022. Classic service metrics.
evaluation_required: Yes — annual data collection activities report
specific_metrics: Prosecutions; prison admissions; UCR data; T-visa data

title: ILO Protocol of 2014 to Convention No. 29 on Forced Labour
summary: Strengthens ILO forced labor convention; victim compensation; prevention; rehabilitation
description: Key international labor trafficking framework. ILO estimates used for global prevalence. Rehabilitation provisions = closest to survivor outcomes in international law.
evaluation_required: Yes — ILO monitoring; national progress reports
specific_metrics: Prosecutions; victim identifications; national action plans

title: DHS Blue Campaign — Public Awareness & Training
summary: Public awareness; materials; training for law enforcement and public; Blue Campaign certification (H.R. 7234 would expand)
description: H.R. 7234 (119th) would add employer certification program to Blue Campaign. Currently: output-only metrics.
specific_metrics: Materials distributed; trainings delivered; public awareness reach

updates.event: DHS Blue Campaign (§434 HSA) introduced / published

title: Enhanced Collaborative Model (ECM) Task Force Program
summary: Multi-agency task forces (LE + victim services); victim-centered approach; sex and labor trafficking
description: NIJ Urban Institute evaluation (2019): 95% sex trafficking investigations → arrest; 77% → prosecution; only 33% under HT statutes. Labor trafficking largely ignored by task forces. Survivor arrest persists.
implementing_agency: DOJ/OVC; BJA; state and local LE; victim service providers
evaluation_required: Yes — PMT and TIMS reporting; NIJ evaluation (Urban Institute)
specific_metrics: Cases opened; arrests; prosecutions; victim services delivered
adaptation_risk: Yes — arrest of trafficking survivors documented as ongoing concern

title: Safe Harbor Laws — National State-Level Aggregate
status: Enacted in majority of states; gaps persist
latest_action: Enacted in majority of states; gaps persist
summary: Non-criminalization of minors for prostitution; mandatory service referrals; trauma-informed care access
description: MN Phase 6 (2025) = best state-level process eval. KY (NIJ) = pre-post system metrics. WA Safe Harbor implementation struggles documented. No state eval measures ≥12-month survivor outcomes.
implementing_agency: State child welfare; juvenile justice; law enforcement
evaluation_required: Yes — state-level evaluations; MN series 2015–2025; KY study
specific_metrics: Arrests/charges of minors; screenings; referrals to CW; service uptake
adaptation_risk: Limited — mandatory court-ordered treatment shown to worsen outcomes (Shared Hope)

updates.event: Enacted — Enacted in majority of states; gaps persist

title: Safe Harbor Laws — State-Level Aggregate (50-State Overview)
identifier: State statutes — e.g., MN Safe Harbor (2011), WA SB 6251 (2010), KY HB 3 (2013)
status: Enacted in majority of states; ongoing implementation and evaluation gaps
latest_action: Enacted in majority of states; ongoing implementation and evaluation gaps
summary: Decriminalizes or diverts minor sex trafficking victims from juvenile justice; mandates services instead of prosecution; varies widely by state on mandatory vs. voluntary treatment
description: MN Safe Harbor Phase 6 (2023–2025): 1,783 initiated services; 2,312 total served; 442 trainings; American Indian, LGBTQ, and youth with disabilities disproportionately represented. No standardized survivor outcome measures. Mandatory treatment element documented as harmful in some studies.
implementing_agency: State child welfare; juvenile justice; law enforcement
evaluation_required: Yes — state-level; MN Safe Harbor Phase 6 eval (2023–2025) most rigorous
specific_metrics: Arrests/charges of minors (reduction); screenings conducted; referrals to child welfare; service uptake; satisfaction scores
survivor_outcomes_possible: Limited — MN Phase 6 tracks satisfaction; no standardized LT outcome measures
adaptation_risk: Limited — mandatory court-ordered treatment shown to worsen outcomes in some evaluations
focus_question: service metrics only at state level; GAP:  no LT survivor outcomes tracked

updates.event: State statutes — e.g., MN Safe Harbor (2011), WA SB 6251 (2010), KY HB 3 (2013) introduced / published
Enacted — Enacted in majority of states; ongoing implementation and evaluation gaps

title: William Wilberforce Trafficking Victims Protection Reauthorization Act of 2008
latest_action: Signed into law by President Bush (P.L. 110-457)
summary: Major TVPA reauthorization significantly expanding protections for unaccompanied immigrant children, creating new child welfare requirements, strengthening civil cause of action for survivors, and expanding international programs.
description: TVPRA 2008 made major structural changes. Key additions: (1) Enhanced protections for unaccompanied alien children (UAC) — required HHS screening before immigration proceedings; (2) Created civil cause of action for trafficking victims against any person who benefited financially (§ 1595); (3) Required FBI to collect annual trafficking data; (4) Mandated federal contractor training; (5) Added provisions on domestic minor sex trafficking (DMST); (6) Strengthened safe harbor for minors by removing requirement that minors prove force/fraud/coercion. Named for British abolitionist William Wilberforce.

updates.notes: Named for British abolitionist William Wilberforce

title: William Wilberforce TVPRA 2008
summary: Strengthens child trafficking provisions; unaccompanied children protections; labor contractor liability
description: Major expansion on child trafficking and unaccompanied minor protections. Requires CPS-style screening for trafficking.
specific_metrics: Cases prosecuted; children screened; T-visas; services delivered

title: New York Safe Harbor Act for Exploited Children
latest_action: Signed into law by Governor Paterson. First safe harbor law in the US.
summary: The first safe harbor law in the United States for commercially sexually exploited minors. Required minors arrested for prostitution to be provided with services rather than prosecuted. Influenced safe harbor legislation in 40+ states.
description: New York's Safe Harbor Act was the first state law in the US to explicitly establish that minors engaged in commercial sex are victims, not criminals. It created a PINS (Person in Need of Supervision) pathway for minors, required access to specialized services, and established the Safe Harbor for Exploited Children Task Force. The model was adopted with variations by 40+ states over the following decade.

updates.event: Bill introduced in New York Assembly
Passed New York Assembly and Senate
Signed into law by Governor David Paterson
updates.notes: First safe harbor law in the United States

title: Unaccompanied Children (UAC) Protections — TVPRA Requirements
summary: UAC transfer to HHS; sponsor vetting; post-release services; 24-hour HHS notification for potential trafficking victims
description: FY2024: 99.4% of children received post-release services. Mar 2025: federal contract for UAC legal services canceled. Feb 2025: fingerprinting required for all sponsors. Significant vulnerability increase.
specific_metrics: Children placed; post-release services received; sponsorship completions

title: TVPA Reauthorization 2003 (TVPRA 2003)
summary: Expands HHS services to family members of foreign nationals; anti-trafficking training; AG annual report
description: Prohibits federal funding to recipients engaged in trafficking. Establishes AG Annual Report.
evaluation_required: Yes — AG annual report; Congressional reporting
specific_metrics: Services expanded; trainings delivered

title: UN Palermo Protocol (Protocol to Prevent, Suppress and Punish Trafficking in Persons)
summary: International definition of trafficking; 3P framework internationally; victim protection obligations
description: FOUNDATIONAL INTERNATIONAL LAW: All U.S. law traces here conceptually. UNODC annual report = global service metrics. Tier-ranking system in TIP Report operationalizes Palermo at bilateral level.
specific_metrics: State party prosecutions; victim identifications; national action plans

title: Trafficking Victims Protection Act of 2000
latest_action: Signed into law by President Clinton (P.L. 106-386)
summary: Landmark legislation establishing the federal framework to combat human trafficking. Defined trafficking in federal law for the first time, created the T visa for survivors, established the OTIP, and authorized funding for the 3P framework: Protection, Prosecution, Prevention.
description: The TVPA 2000 was the first comprehensive federal law addressing human trafficking. Key provisions: (1) Defined 'severe forms of trafficking in persons' covering sex trafficking by force/fraud/coercion and labor trafficking; (2) Created the T nonimmigrant visa for victims; (3) Established the Interagency Task Force to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons; (4) Authorized $95M for FY2001–2003; (5) Criminalized human trafficking under 18 U.S.C. § 1591 (sex trafficking) and § 1589 (forced labor); (6) Created minimum standards for the elimination of trafficking (Tier system). The legislation was championed by a bipartisan coalition and signed as part of the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act.

updates.notes: Introduced as part of broader anti-violence legislation package
Near-unanimous bipartisan passage
Signed as Division A of the Victims of Trafficking and Violence Protection Act of 2000

title: Trafficking Victims Protection Act (TVPA) - Original
summary: Prosecution (18 U.S.C. §1591); protection (T-visa; benefits); prevention (3P framework)
description: ANCHOR LEGISLATION: Establishes 3P framework (prevention, prosecution, protection). All subsequent bills trace to TVPA. Outcome measurement is almost entirely system-level.
policy_mechanism: Criminal Prohibition + Service Funding + Trade Enforcement
evaluation_required: Yes — annual TIP Report; GAO reviews; Congressional reporting
specific_metrics: Prosecutions; convictions; T-visas issued; service utilization; TIP tier rankings
adaptation_risk: Yes — traffickers adapt to enforcement pressure

title: T Nonimmigrant Status (T-Visa) - Immigration Relief
summary: Temporary immigration status for HT victims cooperating with law enforcement; access to benefits
description: Apr 2024 final rule modified T-visa program; clarified reporting/evidentiary requirements. Processing times remain lengthy. Jan 2025: categorical deferred action terminated — fewer protections for some victims.
evaluation_required: Yes — USCIS processing data; T-visa grants reported
specific_metrics: T-visa applications; approvals; denial rates; certified victims

title: Communications Decency Act Section 230 — Baseline Platform Immunity
summary: FOSTA-SESTA carved out trafficking liability from Section 230 immunity; platforms now liable for facilitating prostitution/trafficking
description: Baseline platform law. FOSTA 2018 amendment = key carve-out. Ongoing litigation and advocacy. EARN IT would further modify. Major policy battleground.
specific_metrics: Platform practices; content policies; prosecutions
adaptation_risk: YES — major documented displacement; online trafficking moved to non-U.S. platforms

title: 19 U.S.C. § 1307 - Tariff Act Prohibition on Forced Labor Goods
summary: Prohibits import of goods produced with forced/convict labor; UFLPA builds on this authority
description: Enduring statutory authority. UFLPA operationalizes this for Xinjiang. Key anchor for labor trafficking supply chain track.
evaluation_required: No formal eval; enforcement data to Congress
specific_metrics: Import detentions; values denied; enforcement actions
adaptation_risk: Yes — supply chain displacement well documented

title: 19 U.S.C. § 1307 — Prohibition on Importation of Forced Labor Goods
status: Enacted; foundational supply chain statute; UFLPA (2022) builds on this
latest_action: Enacted; foundational supply chain statute; UFLPA (2022) builds on this
summary: Prohibits importation of goods made with forced or convict labor; CBP authorized to detain/exclude shipments; 'consumptive demand' exception removed 2016
description: Foundational statute for supply chain forced labor enforcement. 2016 TFA amendment removed 'consumptive demand' exception that had blocked enforcement. UFLPA 2022 adds rebuttable presumption for Xinjiang goods. Enforcement metrics only — no survivor impact measurement.
evaluation_required: No formal eval; CBP enforcement data reported to Congress
specific_metrics: Import detentions (count); dollar value of detained/denied goods; enforcement actions; WROs (Withhold Release Orders) issued
adaptation_risk: Yes — documented supply chain displacement to Malaysia, Vietnam, Thailand to evade UFLPA
source: U.S. Tariff Act 1930 (as amended by TFA 2016)

updates.event: Enacted — Enacted; foundational supply chain statute; UFLPA (2022) builds on this

title: Federal Acquisition Regulation (FAR) Anti-Trafficking Provisions
summary: Zero-tolerance for federal contractors; required compliance plans for contracts >$500K; anti-trafficking codes of conduct
description: Compliance/training model. No survivor outcomes. Useful for framing 'adjacent' supply chain mechanism through government contracting.
specific_metrics: Contract compliance certifications; contractor plans; violations reported

title: Human Trafficking Awareness Training Recognition Act of 2026
sponsor: Rep. David G. Valadao [R-CA-22], House Appropriations
cosponsor_list: Valadao also launched bipartisan Combating HT Congressional Caucus March 2026
summary: training/compliance
description: Low
implementing_agency: Blue Campaign certification program for employers who provide HT awareness training; Federal Register recognition; annual reporting
specific_metrics: Yes — employer reporting; Secretary certification
outcome_frame: Employers trained; certificates issued; training hours delivered
focus_question: Training compliance model. No survivor outcomes. Certificate program incentivizes employer training. FQ2 example of output-heavy awareness program.

title: Tariff Act § 307 Withhold Release Orders (WROs) — Ongoing
summary: WROs block import of goods from specific producers; predates UFLPA as enforcement mechanism
description: Enduring tool. Multiple active WROs on various commodities. UFLPA's rebuttable presumption supplements but does not replace WROs for non-XUAR forced labor.
specific_metrics: Shipments detained; goods excluded; WRO compliance
adaptation_risk: Yes — producers shift to evade specific WROs

title: Continued Presence (CP) — Temporary Immigration Status
summary: Quick temporary legal status for trafficking victims cooperating with LE; stabilizes victims during investigation
description: Critical short-term stabilization tool. Pairs with T-visa for longer-term status. No outcome tracking beyond system status.
evaluation_required: Yes — DOJ/DHS annual HT prosecutions report
specific_metrics: CP status granted; victim cooperation rates

title: Office for Victims of Crime (OVC) Anti-Trafficking Direct Services Grants
summary: Direct services to trafficking victims through OVC grantee organizations; comprehensive case management
description: FY2021–22: 16,390 total clients across 192 awards. Classic output reporting model. No survivor outcome tracking beyond service utilization. TIMS = data system.
specific_metrics: Clients served; service types; trainings; new vs. existing clients

title: Anti-Trafficking Information Management System (ATIMS)
summary: Centralized grant recipient reporting database for OTIP programs; aligns TVAP, DVHT, NHTH reporting
description: DATA SYSTEM: ATIMS is the operational backbone for federal HT service data. 2025 PRA updates specifically align TVAP/DVHT forms with ATIMS. System captures outputs, not outcomes.
evaluation_required: Yes — grantee reporting aligned to ATIMS
specific_metrics: Grantee performance data; client demographics; service utilization
